Logline

Hiding from a grief she cannot face, a marine biologist takes a winter posting as the last keeper of a remote Scottish lighthouse, until a stranger's voice reaches her on a radio frequency that shouldn't exist, from a vessel that isn't there.

Gravity × Locke A woman alone · a voice on the radio · a rescue that becomes a reckoning

03The Log, Act One

Black. A radio searching, static, a fragment of coastal forecast, a woman laughing somewhere far off. Then, faint and steady, the slow rhythmic beep of a heart monitor. A girl's voice: "Mum..." The sound cuts. Silence.

Entry IThe Lighthouse

The Lighthouse

Trinity Rock rises out of grey water at dawn , a tower alone on a black rock, miles from any land. Claire Park (42), a marine biologist, is winched up off a heaving boat by the relief keeper, Archie (60s). He hands over fast: lamp on at sundown, off at sunrise; draw the curtains every morning or the lens will burn the tower down; the radio is her only voice to the outside world. He mentions, almost in passing, that the last keeper didn't finish his rotation. Most don't, this far out.

And he tells her the one thing that matters out here: the water has its own mind. Watch what it's actually doing, not what the table says it should. Claire, who lives by measurement, says she doesn't take risks she hasn't measured. Archie almost smiles. "Aye. That's usually who it gets. The ones who measured." Then he's gone, the boat shrinking into the grey, and it's just Claire and the rock. After this rotation the light runs itself. She is the last keeper.

She has come here to disappear. The husband's name lights her phone and she lets it ring out. On the phone, a photograph: a grinning teenage daughter, Grace. And a single voicemail, two years old, that she has never let herself play.

Entry IIThe Voice

The Voice

On her first night the VHF set wakes on a channel she isn't even monitoring. A woman's voice, warm and wry and witty, asks if anyone's out there. She gives her name as Anna, a researcher aboard the RV Enoch, fifty miles past the bank. They talk a few minutes before the signal frays and she's gone.

But when Claire looks afterward, nothing fits. No vessel called Enoch in any registry. The channel assigned to no one. The marine-traffic map shows fifty empty miles in every direction, not a single boat out there at all. She tells herself it's a freak bounce of signal off a warm layer of air, and almost believes it, until she pulls the receiver's own log, and reads the range to source. Zero.

Receiver log · 23:47
Signal strength· · ·
ChannelSCANNING
Range to source· · ·

It came from right here.

Entry IIILow Water

Low Water

She should leave the dead channel dead. Instead, the next night, she turns the set back on and says the name into the static. And Anna answers like no time has passed, "Claire from the rock. You came back."

The radio becomes the shape of Claire's nights. Anna is funny and sharp, Danish, and full of a life still ahead of her: a sister's wedding she's dreading, a man she keeps not-deciding about, a paper she means to finish. For the first time in two years, Claire has someone to talk to. Because a stranger can ask what no one else can, Claire begins, against her own armour, to open up. Even if that someone is a voice that, by every instrument she owns, should not be there.

And there is one rule she cannot explain away. She can only reach Anna at low tide.

Every night she gets through, the water is out; every night the channel is dead, it's high. As if the sea itself decides when the two of them are allowed to speak. Claire keeps answering anyway, a woman who trusts nothing she can't measure, lying awake on a black rock in the dark, the tide already turning, with no answer at all to the only question left.

What is she?
The log breaks off here
The answer is the rest of the film

At the lowest tide of the decade, a path opens across the reef.

To a cave that has been underwater for ten years, and to the truth waiting inside it. The choice it forces on Claire is the heart of the screenplay. The remaining pages are withheld here, by design.

Request the script →
04Characters

One woman on the rock.One voice on the radio.

Plate IOn the rock
Claire Park
42 · the last keeper

A marine biologist who lives by a single creed: "I don't take risks I haven't measured." She took the loneliest posting in the country to be unreachable, to put a cold sea between herself and a grief she can't yet say out loud, and a message she can't bring herself to play. What she came to the rock to avoid is exactly what the voice will make her face.

Plate IIOn the radio
Anna
a voice on the radio

Warm, quick, Danish, quietly brilliant, a researcher mapping a drowned world on the floor of the sea. She is the most alive person Claire has spoken to in two years. She is also, by every instrument on the rock, not there at all. Who she is, and how she's reaching a dead channel, is the question the whole film turns on.

Signal PresentSource · · ·Range 0.0 NM
Plate IIIThe handover
Archie
60s · the relief keeper

Weatherproofed by a life outdoors, and the last man to hand Claire the tower in a hurry , lamp, curtains, radio, and one warning he means more than the rest: watch the water, not the table. Then he's gone, and the rock is hers.

Role HandoverStatus Departed
Plate IVOn the water
?
...

A figure who comes to the rock out of the dark, by a boat that makes no sound, and is gone before Claire can be sure she saw him at all. He wants something on Trinity Rock, but what he is to her, and what he knows, the rock keeps to itself. For now.

Identity · · ·Intent Unknown
05The Deep, world & science
One speculative leap, hung on real science

Anna's work is real: paleo-acoustic mapping of Doggerland, the drowned prehistoric country beneath the North Sea.

You fire sound down into the seabed and read what comes back; build the echoes up and you draw a land nobody has seen in millennia. And out here the ground holds onto sound longer than it has any right to. Every so often it gives you back something you never sent, an echo that comes home late.

The film makes a single, quiet extension of a genuine and still-unexplained phenomenon, and never over-explains it. The strange half works because the real half is real.

06Author's statement
The emotional core

The Last Keeper is about the people we couldn't be there for, and the people we still can.

Claire Park was at sea when her daughter died. Grace had been ill for two years, but her condition was stable and the doctors had cleared the trip, Claire believed there was time. Then a storm knocked out the boat's phone, leaving her unreachable for four days. By the time she had a signal again, it was over. All that was left was a voicemail from Grace she didn't hear until she was already back on land, a call she might have answered, now only a recording. She's carried it in her pocket, unplayed, for two years.

There are thousands of versions of Claire in the world: people who were on the wrong continent, in the wrong meeting, on the wrong side of a door, when the person they loved most needed them.

Why now

Everyone carries a version of the same sentence. If I'd just called. If I'd just been there. If I'd just caught it in time. We replay the one decision we believe we could have made differently, in a loop for years.

The Last Keeper is for everyone who has lain awake inside that loop. It's about the most relatable lie there is, that if we'd only done the one thing, we could have stopped it, and about the hard freedom of finally putting it down.

What I promise

A film that is patient but never slack. A mystery that pays off in feeling rather than mechanism. A genre engine, radio, tide, storm, a closing deadline, strong enough to hold an audience that didn't come for a meditation on grief, and an emotional core honest enough not to insult the one that did. Gravity's containment and Locke's intimacy, on a single rock in the Scottish sea.

- Danny Lee
